issues.description: Both endpoints (PATCH / and GET /:memberId) have @x-autobe-authorization-type null and no authentication guard. The member list endpoint exposes email addresses, account status, and creation dates of all members. The GET endpoint exposes full member profiles. These are sensitive administrative operations with no access control.
issues.suggestion: Apply a NestJS authentication guard (e.g., @UseGuards(MemberAuthGuard)) to both endpoints. The list endpoint in particular should require admin-level permissions since it exposes all member accounts.

issues.description: The provider updates a project member record using only the employeeId and projectId from the path parameters without verifying that the requesting member has permission to modify this project, that the project belongs to the requesting member's organization, or that the employee belongs to the same organization. There is no authorization check at all before performing the update.
issues.suggestion: Before performing the update, verify: (1) the project exists and belongs to the requesting member's organization, (2) the requesting member has project_management permission, (3) the target employee belongs to the same organization. Throw 403/404 as appropriate.

issues.description: The profile update provider returns `typia.random<IHrmTimeTrackUserProfile>()` — completely random data — instead of actually updating the profile. This is a stub that was never implemented. Any caller will receive fabricated data, and no actual update occurs, silently discarding user input.
issues.suggestion: Implement the actual profile update logic: validate the member's identity, update the hrm_time_track_user_profiles record for the authenticated member, and return the updated profile. Remove the typia.random() stub.

issues.description: The snapshot list and detail endpoints use @MemberAuth() for authentication but the specification notes 'Validate that timerId exists and belongs to the requesting employee's organization' — this ownership check is delegated entirely to the provider. If the provider does not enforce this, any authenticated member could access timer snapshots belonging to other organizations.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider (patchHrmTimeTrackMemberTimersTimerIdSnapshots and getHrmTimeTrackMemberTimersTimerIdSnapshotsSnapshotId) explicitly filters by the authenticated member's organization_id when querying timers and snapshots.

issues.description: All timelog endpoints (create, list, get, update, delete) have @x-autobe-authorization-type null despite handling sensitive work-time data. While @MemberAuth() is present for authentication, there is no RBAC guard enforcing the permission checks described in the specifications (e.g., 'time management permission', 'time:view_all'). These checks are entirely delegated to providers with no controller-level enforcement.
issues.suggestion: Consider adding role-based guards at the controller level for sensitive operations (update, delete). At minimum, ensure providers consistently enforce the described permission checks and ownership rules.

issues.description: All role permission management endpoints (add, update, get, delete) have @x-autobe-authorization-type null. Role permission management is a highly privileged operation that should require organization_management permission. While @MemberAuth() provides authentication, there is no guard enforcing that the caller has the required elevated permissions at the controller level.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission guard requiring 'organization_management' permission for POST, PATCH, and DELETE operations on role permissions. The GET operation may be less sensitive but should still verify organization membership.

issues.description: The session list provider accepts member_id and organization_id as optional filters from the request body but does not enforce that the requesting member can only view their own sessions. A member could pass any member_id to view another member's sessions, including IP addresses, href, and referrer data.
issues.suggestion: Unless the requesting member has admin privileges, restrict the query to only return sessions belonging to props.member.id. If cross-member session viewing is needed, add an explicit permission check before allowing the member_id filter to be applied.

issues.description: The guest join endpoint creates member accounts, employee records, and organization memberships based solely on an invitation token. If the token validation in the provider is weak (e.g., predictable tokens, no expiry enforcement, no single-use guarantee), this could allow unauthorized account creation and organization access.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider uses cryptographically secure invitation tokens, enforces single-use semantics (mark as 'accepted' atomically), validates expiry strictly, and verifies the email in the request matches the invited email exactly.

issues.description: Authentication endpoints (login, join, refresh) use console.log(error) for error handling. Failed login attempts, invalid tokens, and registration failures are not structured or audited. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but worth noting for auth-sensitive endpoints.
issues.suggestion: Consider implementing structured audit logging for authentication events (login success/failure, token refresh, registration) to support security monitoring and incident response.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to authentication endpoints (login, join, refresh). This allows brute-force attacks against credentials and token endpoints.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting middleware (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to authentication endpoints, especially login and token refresh.

issues.description: Password complexity validation is not enforced at the controller level. The specification mentions 'password strength requirements' but this is delegated entirely to the provider with no visible validation.
issues.suggestion: Add explicit password complexity validation (minimum length, character requirements) using typia constraints or a dedicated validation pipe on the IJoin body.

issues.description: No organization-level ownership check when fetching a role snapshot by ID. Any authenticated member can retrieve any role snapshot by guessing or knowing a valid snapshotId UUID, regardless of whether it belongs to their organization.
issues.suggestion: After fetching the snapshot, verify that the associated role belongs to the same organization as the requesting member. Join through the role to the organization and compare with the member's organization.

issues.description: No authorization check before creating a permission on a role snapshot. Any authenticated member can add permissions to any role snapshot by providing a valid snapshotId, without verifying they belong to the same organization or have admin/manager privileges.
issues.suggestion: Verify that the snapshot belongs to the requesting member's organization and that the member has sufficient privileges (e.g., role management permission) before allowing permission creation.

issues.description: The project membership check uses `props.member.id` (the member/user ID) as `hrm_time_track_employee_id`, but these are likely different entity types. If member ID and employee ID are distinct, this check will always fail or incorrectly pass, bypassing the intended access control.
issues.suggestion: First resolve the member's employee ID for the relevant organization, then use that employee ID to check `hrm_time_track_project_members`. Do not use `props.member.id` directly as an employee ID.

issues.description: The authorization query uses `id: record.id` (the timesheet's ID) to match against an employee's ID in the OR clause. This is a logic error — a timesheet ID will never equal an employee ID, so the first OR branch is always false. Only the permission-based branch can grant access, potentially allowing any member with `time:approve` permission to view any timesheet.
issues.suggestion: Replace `id: record.id` with the actual employee ID from the timesheet record (e.g., `record.hrm_time_track_employee_id`) to correctly allow employees to view their own timesheets.

issues.description: No role/permission check before creating a department. Any authenticated member can create departments in their organization without requiring admin or manager-level permissions.
issues.suggestion: Check that the requesting member has a specific permission (e.g., `org:manage` or `department:create`) before allowing department creation.

issues.description: No role/permission check before creating an employee record. Any authenticated member can add new employees to their organization without requiring elevated privileges.
issues.suggestion: Verify the requesting member has an appropriate permission (e.g., `employee:create` or HR admin role) before allowing employee creation.

issues.description: No role/permission check before creating a project. Any authenticated member can create projects in their organization's session context without requiring manager or admin privileges.
issues.suggestion: Verify the requesting member has a project creation permission before allowing this operation.

issues.description: The `sortBy` field from user input is used directly as a dynamic object key in `orderByInput` without validation against an allowlist. This could allow unexpected sort fields to be injected into the Prisma query.
issues.suggestion: Validate `sortBy` against an explicit allowlist of permitted sort fields (e.g., `['created_at', 'updated_at', 'status']`) before using it as a dynamic key.

issues.description: Token refresh failures (invalid tokens, expired sessions, deleted accounts) are not logged. This makes it difficult to detect token abuse or brute-force attempts against the refresh endpoint.
issues.suggestion: Add structured logging for authentication failures including the reason and a sanitized identifier (e.g., hashed token prefix) to support security monitoring.

issues.description: Refresh tokens are not rotated on use — the same refresh token remains valid after issuing new tokens. This means a stolen refresh token can be reused indefinitely until session expiry.
issues.suggestion: Implement refresh token rotation: invalidate the current refresh token on use and issue a new one. Consider storing a token version or nonce in the session to detect reuse of old tokens.

issues.description: Rate limiting is not implemented on this or other endpoints. Consider adding rate limiting at the infrastructure or middleware level.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting using NestJS ThrottlerModule or an API gateway to prevent abuse.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/139 files] [2 chunks, 22→22 issues] The codebase has several significant security issues. Most critically: (1) the member list and detail endpoints have no authentication guard, exposing all member accounts publicly; (2) the project member update provider has no authorization checks whatsoever; (3) the profile update provider is an unimplemented stub returning random data. Additionally, the session list provider allows cross-member data access, role permission management lacks RBAC enforcement at the controller level, and authentication endpoints lack rate limiting and structured audit logging. The use of @MemberAuth() on most member-scoped endpoints is positive, but the delegation of all authorization logic to providers without controller-level guards creates risk if any provider has gaps.

issues.description: Function returns `typia.random<IHrmTimeTrackUserProfile>()`, which is a placeholder/stub that does not implement any actual update logic. This is a stub or incomplete implementation.
issues.suggestion: Implement actual update logic for user profile, similar to other PUT endpoints - query the profile, update based on body, and return transformed result.

issues.description: Organization update endpoint lacks authorization checks (missing member organization ownership/permission validation). Any member can update any organization.
issues.suggestion: Add authorization check: verify member is owner/admin of the organization before allowing update.

issues.description: Project update endpoint only verifies that project belongs to member's organization, but doesn't check if member has project management permission.
issues.suggestion: Add permission check: verify member has project_management role or is project-lead before allowing update.

issues.description: Project member update endpoint has no authorization checks - any member can update any project member.
issues.suggestion: Add authorization check: verify member has project_management permission or is project-lead before updating project members.

issues.description: Authorization check compares `timesheet.hrm_time_track_employee_id === props.member.id`, but `props.member.id` is likely member ID (not employee ID). This will fail because IDs are from different tables.
issues.suggestion: Fetch member's employee record in the organization first, then compare `timesheet.hrm_time_track_employee_id === employee.id`.

issues.description: Employee query uses `where: { id: props.member.id }`, but member ID is not employee ID. Should query employee by `hrm_time_track_member_id`.
issues.suggestion: Change query to `where: { hrm_time_track_member_id: props.member.id }`.

issues.description: Unused imports: `IHrmTimeTrackDepartment`, `IHrmTimeTrackMember`, `IHrmTimeTrackOrganization`, `IHrmTimeTrackRole`, `IHrmTimeTrackTimelog`, `IHrmTimeTrackTimesheet`, `ArrayUtil`, `jwt`, `PasswordUtil`, `toISOStringSafe`. Similar unused imports appear in multiple files.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up code.

issues.description: Importing non-existent interfaces: IHrmTimeTrackDepartment, IHrmTimeTrackEmployee, IHrmTimeTrackMember, IHrmTimeTrackOrganization, IHrmTimeTrackProject, IHrmTimeTrackRole, IHrmTimeTrackTask are imported but not used in the function. These are likely hallucinated imports.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across files: Many files import interfaces that are not used (e.g., IHrmTimeTrackDepartment, IHrmTimeTrackEmployee, IHrmTimeTrackMember, IHrmTimeTrackOrganization, IHrmTimeTrackProject, IHrmTimeTrackRole, IHrmTimeTrackTask). This pattern appears in multiple files: postHrmTimeTrackMemberTimelogs.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberTasks.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberProjectsProjectIdMembers.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberProjects.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberEmployeesEmployeeIdContracts.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberEmployees.ts, postHrmTimeTrackMemberDepartments.ts, patchHrmTimeTrackMemberTimesheetsTimesheetIdTimelogs.ts.
issues.suggestion: Audit all imports and remove those that are not referenced in the file.

issues.description: Incorrect relationship mapping: The function passes `hrmTimeTrackEmployees: { id: props.member.id }` but `props.member.id` is the member ID, not the employee ID. According to requirements, Timelog belongs to Employee, not directly to Member. This creates a data integrity issue.
issues.suggestion: Fetch the employee ID associated with the member in the current organization context before creating the timelog.

issues.description: Missing organization context validation: The task creation does not validate that the member has permission to create tasks in the project's organization. It should check the member's session organization against the project's organization.
issues.suggestion: Add validation to ensure the member belongs to the same organization as the project before creating a task.

issues.description: Incorrect Prisma query structure: The session query uses `organization: { select: { id: true } }` but the session model likely has a direct field `hrm_time_track_organization_id`. This may cause a runtime error.
issues.suggestion: Change the query to select `hrm_time_track_organization_id` directly from the session.

issues.description: Hardcoded organization selection: The code takes the first organization from the member's employee relationships without considering the current session context. This may not align with the multi-organization requirement.
issues.suggestion: Use the organization from the session context or allow the member to specify which organization they are logging into.

issues.description: Unrealistic assumption about invitation token: The code compares `guest.id !== props.body.invitationToken`, assuming the invitation token is the guest ID. This may not match the actual token field in the guest model.
issues.suggestion: Check the correct token field (e.g., `token` column) in the guest model for validation.

issues.description: Unused imports: The file imports many dependencies (IEntity, ArrayUtil, HttpException, Prisma, jwt, typia, v4, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe) but only uses HttpException. This is dead code.
issues.suggestion: Remove all unused imports.

issues.description: Function name 'patchHrmTimeTrackMemberTimesheetsTimesheetIdSnapshots' suggests a PATCH operation but the function implements a GET/list operation with pagination and filtering. This is a requirements mismatch - PATCH should update resources, not list them.
issues.suggestion: Rename function to 'getHrmTimeTrackMemberTimesheetsTimesheetIdSnapshots' or implement actual PATCH logic to update timesheet snapshots.

issues.description: Incorrect authorization logic: The WHERE clause filters tasks where project has projectMembers with hrm_time_track_employee_id equal to props.member.id, but member.id is a member ID, not an employee ID. This will likely return no results.
issues.suggestion: Change 'hrm_time_track_employee_id: props.member.id' to look up the employee ID from the member's employee record first.

issues.description: Authorization logic assumes member session has hrm_time_track_organization_id, but according to requirements, User belongs to multiple Organizations. The session should be scoped to a specific organization, but the code doesn't verify the member has access to that organization's timelogs.
issues.suggestion: Add verification that the member has an employee record in the session's organization before allowing access to timelogs.

issues.description: Incomplete authorization logic: The comment mentions 'check for time:approve permission' but the code only throws 403 if no employee record exists. Permission checking is not implemented.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper permission checking based on role permissions for viewing other employees' timesheets.

issues.description: Assumes member has exactly one employee record (employees[0]) and throws 403 if not. According to requirements, User belongs to multiple Organizations, so a member could have multiple employee records across organizations.
issues.suggestion: Handle multiple organizations by filtering projects by the organization context from the session or allowing member to specify which organization to query.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across all files: IEntity, IHrmTimeTrackDepartment, IHrmTimeTrackEmployee, IHrmTimeTrackMember, IHrmTimeTrackOrganization, IHrmTimeTrackProject, IHrmTimeTrackRole, IHrmTimeTrackTask, jwt, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe, v4. These imports are not used in the functions.
issues.suggestion: Remove all unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: Copy-paste error in orderByInput: When sortBy is 'total_hours', it uses { id: sortOrder } instead of a proper total_hours field. This suggests the code was copied from another entity without proper adaptation.
issues.suggestion: Implement proper sorting for total_hours if the field exists, or remove this case if not applicable.
